prompt_md: Given `template <typename T> struct Pair { T first; T second; };`, why does the line `Pair p{ 1, 2 };` fail to compile (without CTAD)?

options.a: Because `Pair` is a template, not a type, so the compiler needs `Pair<int>` to know which instantiation to use.
options.b: Because aggregate initialization is not allowed for templated structs.
options.c: Because the template parameter `T` cannot be deduced as `int` from integer literals.
options.d: Because `1` and `2` need to be the same type for `Pair` to compile.

explanation_md: A class template is a recipe for generating types, not a type itself. Without explicit template arguments the compiler has no type to substitute for `T` and reports `missing template arguments before 'p'`. Aggregate initialization works fine on instantiated templates, and although the next lesson introduces CTAD, by default the brackets are required.

`Box<int>` instantiates a concrete struct with one `int` member named `value`. `a.value` is 10 and `b.value` is 32, so the sum printed is 42. Both variables share the same instantiated type because they use the same template argument.

prompt_md: Why does a class template usually need to be defined in a header file rather than a `.cpp` file?

options.a: Header files compile faster than `.cpp` files.
options.b: The compiler must see the full template body at every point of use to instantiate it for the specific type argument.
options.d: Linkers refuse to link any symbol whose definition lives in a `.cpp` file.

explanation_md: Class templates are stencils — the compiler produces concrete code only when it sees both the template definition and the type argument together. If the template lives only in a `.cpp` file, other translation units that use it see no definition to instantiate from, and the resulting symbols never get generated, leading to a linker error.

options.a: Aggregate initialization with braces is not allowed inside `main`.
options.b: `Pair<int>` and `Pair<long>` are unrelated types — there is no implicit conversion between different instantiations.
options.c: `first` and `second` cannot be the same type.
options.d: The template definition is missing a `typename` keyword inside the body.

explanation_md: Different instantiations of the same template are different types. `Pair<int>` and `Pair<long>` share the source recipe but the type system treats them as unrelated, just as `int` and `long` are unrelated. Copying one into the other requires explicitly constructing the target type member-by-member.

`Tagged<int, char>` instantiates a struct where `value` is `int` and `tag` is `char`. `e.tag` prints the character `X` and `e.value` prints `7`; without any separator they appear adjacent on one line.
